question: how to measure soil biology
title: How to Measure Soil Biology: Tests That Actually Tell You What's Alive in Your Ground
metaDescription: Standard soil tests tell you about chemistry. Measuring soil biology is a different animal — and it's what actually matters for building living soil. Scotty explains the Haney test, aggregate stability, and the low-tech methods that work in any backyard.
content:
# How to Measure Soil Biology: Tests That Actually Tell You What's Alive in Your Ground

Y'all, most of the soil tests people use were designed to tell you how much nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium to add to your soil. They're chemistry tests. They answer the question: what's the nutritional status of this dirt?

That's a useful question, but it's not the question I care most about. The question I care about is: is this soil alive? Is the decay cycle running? Is there a biological community here that's doing the work of nutrient cycling, building structure, and feeding plants the way nature intended?

Those questions require different tests. And some of them don't require any lab at all.

## What Standard Soil Tests Don't Tell You

Let me start with what standard tests miss, because I think it's important to understand the limitation before we talk about solutions.

A standard Mehlich-3 or Morgan soil test will give you numbers for pH, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, magnesium, and a few other minerals. It will tell you cation exchange capacity — essentially the soil's capacity to hold nutrients. It might tell you organic matter percentage as a broad metric.

What it won't tell you is whether the biology is active enough to make those nutrients available. A soil can have adequate phosphorus in the mineral form but near-zero biological activity, which means the phosphorus is locked up in forms plants can't access. The chemistry looks fine on paper. The plants still can't thrive.

Conversely, a soil with rich biology and high organic matter can produce outstanding plant growth with measured nutrient levels that look low to a conventional agronomist, because the biology is cycling nutrients faster than the static snapshot of the test captures.

This is the limitation of chemistry-only testing: it measures what's there, but not whether the living system can use it.

## The Haney Test — Measuring Biology Through Activity

The test that has gotten the most attention in regenerative agriculture circles for measuring soil biological health is the Haney test, developed by Dr. Rick Haney at the USDA Agricultural Research Service in Temple, Texas — right here in our neck of the woods.

The Haney test is fundamentally different from standard chemistry tests because it measures biological activity. Specifically, it measures soil respiration: how much carbon dioxide the microbial community in your soil produces over a 24-hour period after the soil is dried and then rewet.

Here's the key concept: when you rewet dry soil, you trigger a burst of microbial activity. The bacteria and fungi wake up and start metabolizing. The amount of CO2 they produce in that first 24 hours is a direct indicator of microbial biomass and activity. More CO2 means more active biology.

The Noble Research Institute describes the Haney test as integrating chemical and biological measurements to assess nutrient status, microbial biomass, and aspects of the microbial habitat to determine overall soil health. It also measures what's called microbially active carbon — the fraction of total organic carbon that's readily available as microbial food. This is like measuring how full the pantry is for the bacterial community.

The result is a soil health score that reflects not just what's chemically present but whether the biology can actually deliver it to plants. Farmers using the Haney test alongside standard tests often find they can reduce synthetic fertilizer inputs significantly because the biological system, when accounted for, is doing more nutrient delivery than the chemistry-only view suggested.

## Aggregate Stability — The Physical Sign of Biological Health

Here's a test you can do at home in about ten minutes with a jar of water and some soil. It's one of my favorites because it's so immediate and visual.

Fill a jar about halfway with water. Drop a small clump of your soil in. Watch what happens.

If the soil clump stays largely intact for several minutes — if it holds its shape before slowly softening and settling — that's a sign of good aggregate stability. If it immediately clouds the water and disintegrates on contact, you've got a soil with poor structure and low biological activity.

This is the slake test, and what it's measuring is the stability of soil aggregates. Aggregates are clumps of soil particles bonded together by microbial activity — specifically by the sticky compounds that bacteria and fungi produce as byproducts of their metabolism. Healthy living soil has lots of aggregates because the biology is constantly producing the biological glue that holds them together.

I talk about aggregates all the time when people ask me what living soil looks like. Take a handful of your soil and look at it closely. Can you see clumps? Are there little ball-shaped or irregular clusters of particles that hold together when you gently squeeze them? Those are aggregates. They're made of organic matter, mineral particles, and biological compounds — held together by the secretions of the microbial community.

A soil without aggregates — dusty, powdery, or compacted — is telling you the biology isn't working. The decay cycle isn't running properly. The organisms that should be producing those binding compounds aren't there.

Formal aggregate stability testing uses wet sieving — placing soil on a mesh in water and measuring how much holds together versus how much washes through. But the jar test gives you a rapid qualitative read that's perfectly adequate for garden-scale assessment.

## The Smell Test — Geosmin and What It Tells You

Here's one that requires no equipment at all: smell your soil.

Healthy living soil has a distinct earthy smell. That smell comes from a compound called geosmin, produced by actinobacteria — a group of bacteria that are particularly important in the decomposition of complex organic compounds. If your soil smells like fresh earth after rain, you've got actinobacteria working in there.

Soil that smells like nothing — dusty, mineral, flat — typically indicates low biological activity. Soil that smells sour, like vinegar or sour milk, often indicates anaerobic conditions: too much compaction, poor drainage, and biology that's shifted to anaerobic fermentation rather than aerobic decomposition. That's not what you want in garden soil.

The smell test is crude, but it's real. It took Albert Howard decades of careful observation to describe what he observed in fertile Indian soils, but the farmers he observed knew by smell and texture and appearance whether their soil was in good condition. The biology communicates through the senses as well as through lab instruments.

## Soil CO2 Respiration — The Quickest Biological Snapshot

Beyond the Haney test, soil respiration can be measured at home with relatively simple equipment. Some garden supply companies sell CO2 respiration kits that let you measure microbial activity directly. You moisten a soil sample, seal it in a container, and measure CO2 production over 24 to 48 hours.

The principle is simple: the more CO2, the more microbial activity, the more biology is present and working. A soil that produces high CO2 on a respiration test has an active, functioning biological community. A soil that produces very little CO2 is biologically dormant or dead.

The University of Minnesota Extension and ATTRA Sustainable Agriculture both describe respiration as one of the most important biological indicators in soil health assessment. It's fast, measurable, and directly tied to the biological functions that make soil productive.

## What I Look For in My Own Soil

Let me tell you what I actually do in my backyard and at the Needville project, because I think practical examples are more useful than abstract frameworks.

I use the jar test regularly when I'm assessing a new area or trying to track progress. I squeeze handfuls of soil to feel for aggregates. I smell it. I look for earthworm activity — earthworms are a good indicator species for biological health because they need moisture, organic matter, and an active microbial community to thrive.

Every few years I run a Haney test on soil I've been building, because I want to see whether the biological activity is improving over time. The numbers tell me whether the decay cycle is running properly and whether the inputs I'm adding — compost, cover crops, organic matter — are translating into a more active biological community.

The goal isn't to hit a specific number. The goal is to build a trend — a soil that's getting more biologically active over time, that's accumulating organic matter, that's building structure. The numbers confirm what the physical tests and the plant performance are already telling me.

Measuring soil biology is how you know whether you're succeeding at regenerative practice. The chemistry tells you what's there. The biology tells you whether the system is working.

---

- [Evaluation of Aggregate Stability Methods for Soil Health](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0016706122004633)
- [Soil Health Indicators and Tests — ATTRA Sustainable Agriculture](https://attra.ncat.org/publication/soil-health-indicators-and-tests/)
- [Haney Test Interpretation Guide — Ward Laboratories](https://www.wardlab.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/09/Haney-Rev-1.0-Interpretation-Guide.pdf)

Evaluation of Aggregate Stability Methods for Soil Health

scottyQuotes: Standard soil tests measure chemistry. Living soil is biology. Those are different questions that need different tests.
Drop a clump of your soil in a jar of water. If it holds together, you've got aggregates — the biological glue of living soil. If it clouds the water immediately, your decay cycle isn't running.
Smell your soil. If it smells like fresh earth after rain, you've got actinobacteria working. That earthy smell is geosmin. It's the smell of life.
